data.title: Dropdown uses controlled-only API
data.hypothesis: A controlled-only API avoids SSR hydration mismatches because the component never renders DOM until client-side JavaScript initializes and takes ownership of the state.
data.context: Dropdown was initially implemented as an uncontrolled component. SSR hydration bugs caused the dropdown to flash open on page load and mismatched selected values between server and client renders.

alternatives.reasonRejected: Native select cannot be styled consistently across platforms. Blade targets both web and React Native, and native select rendering differs significantly across browsers and platforms.
Complex state management required to detect hydration and suppress flash. Multiple React 17 issues with ref-based hydration detection reported in the community.
Increased API surface and testing complexity. The team decided a single pattern was more maintainable than supporting both modes.

data.chosenSolution: Dropdownexposes a controlled-only API where the parent component manages selection state via `value` and `onChange` props. The component never maintains internal state for the selected value.
tradeOffs: Consumers must always manage selection state — no 'drop-in' usage for simple cases
Re-render performance depends on parent's state management, not the component's internal optimization
Clearer mental model: the component is always a controlled input, never a hybrid
Easier to test: all behavior is driven by props, no internal state branches

evidence.title: feat(Dropdown): controlled-only API
evidence.excerpt: We chose controlled because uncontrolled causes hydration mismatches in SSR. The flash of incorrect state on page load is unacceptable for a primary navigation component.

evidence.title: SSR hydration mismatch in Dropdown component
evidence.excerpt: Dropdown flashes open on every page load because the server renders with default state and client hydrates with a different state. This is a known React 17 pattern but we should fix it at the component level.

data.expectedOutcome: Eliminate SSR hydration mismatches across all Dropdown instances in Blade.
data.observedOutcome: Hydration mismatches were eliminated. Some teams reported increased boilerplate for simple selectors, but the trade-off was accepted as documented.
data.confidenceRationale: The decision is explicitly stated in PR #1423 with clear reasoning. The supporting issue #89 documents the concrete problem. Both evidence items have verbatim excerpts that directly support the claim.

data.title: Dropdown accessibility API v2
data.hypothesis: A separate, explicit accessibility API will make screen reader support more predictable than relying on ARIA attributes inferred from the controlled API pattern.
data.context: After the controlled-only API shipped, accessibility audits revealed that screen readers were not announcing dropdown state changes reliably. The existing ARIA attribute inference was fragile and inconsistent across browsers.

alternatives.option: Fix ARIA inference in the existing API
alternatives.reasonRejected: The inference logic was complex and browser-specific. Maintaining per-browser ARIA quirks in the component itself was not sustainable.
Added bundle size and dependency risk. The team preferred a built-in solution that could be documented and tested as part of Blade's own accessibility testing pipeline.

data.chosenSolution: Introduced explicit `accessibility` prop object with `label`, `description`, `role` fields. Screen reader announcements are now deterministic and testable via the component's own test suite.
tradeOffs: Additional API surface — consumers must learn the accessibility prop pattern
More verbose usage for simple cases that worked with default ARIA
Predictable screen reader behavior across all supported browsers
Accessibility behavior is now testable in component tests, not just E2E

evidence.title: feat(Dropdown): explicit accessibility API
evidence.excerpt: The existing ARIA inference was fragile. We need an explicit API that gives consumers control over screen reader announcements. This also lets us test accessibility behavior in unit tests.

evidence.title: SSR hydration mismatch in Dropdown component
evidence.excerpt: Screen readers not announcing dropdown state changes after the controlled-only migration. The ARIA live region inference doesn't work consistently in Firefox.

data.expectedOutcome: Consistent screen reader announcements across Chrome, Firefox, and Safari.
data.confidenceRationale: The PR is explicit about the decision, but there is no follow-up issue or PR confirming the outcome. The evidence supports the motivation but not the result.

data.title: Native select rejected for Dropdown
data.hypothesis: Using the native HTML select element would eliminate the need for a custom dropdown implementation, reducing maintenance burden and ensuring consistent behavior across platforms.
data.context: During initial architecture discussions for Blade's Dropdown component, the team evaluated whether a native select element would suffice before building a custom solution.
data.chosenSolution: Native select was rejected. Blade targets both web and React Native. A native select would require a separate implementation per platform, defeating the purpose of a cross-platform design system component.
tradeOffs: Cross-platform consistency required a custom solution
Native select styling limitations made it unusable for the design system's visual requirements
Custom dropdown added significant engineering investment but enabled platform-consistent behavior

evidence.title: feat(Dropdown): controlled-only API
evidence.excerpt: We considered native select but it can't be styled consistently across platforms. Since Blade targets both web and React Native, we need a custom solution that works identically everywhere.

evidence.title: RFC: Dropdown component — platform considerations
evidence.excerpt: Given our cross-platform requirements, a native select would need separate implementations for web and mobile. The proposal is to build a custom dropdown that renders consistently everywhere.

data.expectedOutcome: A custom dropdown that provides consistent behavior and appearance across all platforms.
data.confidenceRationale: Both the PR and the RFC explicitly document the native select rejection with cross-platform reasoning. The evidence is strong and consistent.

decision:razorpay/blade:alert:action-slo.label: Alert action slots instead of props

data.title: Alert action slots instead of props
data.hypothesis: Using named slots for actions (primary action, secondary action, dismiss) will be more flexible than boolean props for different action combinations.
data.context: Alert component needed to support multiple action patterns: single CTA, primary+secondary, dismiss only, and custom action content. Boolean props like `showDismiss` and `showPrimaryAction` were becoming unwieldy.

alternatives.reasonRejected: Prop combinations grew combinatorially. Six boolean props would create 64 possible configurations, many invalid. TypeScript couldn't track valid combinations.

data.chosenSolution: Named slot pattern: `primaryAction`, `secondaryAction`, and `dismiss` slots that consumers fill with button components. Invalid combinations are impossible because slots are optional by design.
tradeOffs: More verbose — consumers must import and render Button components
Maximum flexibility — any action pattern is supported without new props
TypeScript tracks valid states through component composition, not prop validation

evidence.title: feat(Alert): action slot pattern
evidence.excerpt: Boolean props for alert actions have become unmanageable. The slot pattern lets consumers compose exactly what they need without us maintaining combinatorial prop validation.

data.expectedOutcome: Eliminate invalid action configurations at the type level.
data.observedOutcome: Action slot pattern was successful but later superseded by a more general composition API in Alert v3.
data.confidenceRationale: The PR documents the decision, but there is limited discussion evidence showing the alternatives were deeply evaluated.

data.title: Alert v3 composition API
data.hypothesis: A full composition API (Alert.Root, Alert.Title, Alert.Description, Alert.Actions) will be more extensible than named slots as the component adds more features.
data.context: Alert v2 with named slots worked well for actions but didn't scale to new features: custom icons, collapsible content, inline links, and toast mode. Each new feature required a new slot or prop.

alternatives.option: Extend slot pattern with more slots
alternatives.reasonRejected: Adding slots for every new feature would lead to the same combinatorial complexity as props. The slot pattern was an intermediate step, not the final API.

data.chosenSolution: Compound component pattern: Alert.Root (container + context), Alert.Title, Alert.Description, Alert.Actions, Alert.Icon. Each sub-component is independently configurable. Context via React context, not prop drilling.
tradeOffs: Extremely flexible — any layout or feature combination is supported
Tree-shakeable — consumers only pay for sub-components they use
Learning curve: consumers must understand the compound component pattern

evidence.title: feat(Alert): compound component API v3
evidence.excerpt: The slot pattern hit its limits. Compound components give us infinite extensibility without combinatorial props. Each new feature becomes a sub-component, not a prop or slot.

data.expectedOutcome: Alert component can be extended with new features without API breaking changes.
data.confidenceRationale: The PR explicitly documents the reasoning for the architecture change, cites the slot pattern limitations, and the compound component pattern is a well-established React pattern.

data.title: SSR hydration mismatch in Dropdown component
data.author: rahulghadge

data.message: feat(Dropdown): implement controlled-only API with SSR guard
data.author: rahulghadge

AFFECTS:decision:razorpay/blade:dropdown.rationale: This decision directly shapes the Dropdown component's API surface.
The a11y API v2 decision directly shapes Dropdown's accessibility surface.

AFFECTS:decision:razorpay/blade:alert:ac.rationale: The action slot pattern defined how Alert actions work in v2.

AFFECTS:decision:razorpay/blade:alert:co.rationale: The composition API replaced the slot pattern as Alert's architecture.

SUPERSEDES:decision:razorpay/blade:dropd.rationale: The v2 a11y API supersedes the original controlled-only API by adding explicit accessibility controls that the first version lacked.

SUPERSEDES:decision:razorpay/blade:alert.rationale: The compound component API in Alert v3 replaced the named slot pattern from v2, providing more extensibility.

SUPPORTED_BY:decision:razorpay/blade:dro.rationale: PR #1423 implements and documents the controlled-only API decision.
Issue #89 documents the SSR hydration problem that motivated the decision.

REJECTED_ALTERNATIVE:decision:razorpay/b.rationale: Issue #45 documents the RFC where native select was rejected in favor of a custom solution.

SUPPORTED_BY:decision:razorpay/blade:dro.rationale: PR #2101 implements the explicit a11y API.
Issue #89 also documents the a11y concerns that motivated the v2 API.

IMPLEMENTS:pull_request:razorpay/blade#1.rationale: PR #1423 contains the code changes that implement the controlled-only API.

IMPLEMENTS:pull_request:razorpay/blade#2.rationale: PR #2101 contains the code that implements the a11y v2 API.

IMPLEMENTS:commit:razorpay/blade@a1b2c3d.rationale: The commit contains the implementation of the controlled-only Dropdown API.

DISCUSSED_IN:decision:razorpay/blade:dro.rationale: The SSR hydration issue was discussed in Issue #89 before the decision was made.

SUPPORTED_BY:decision:razorpay/blade:dro.rationale: PR #1423 documents the native select rejection with cross-platform reasoning.
Issue #45 contains the RFC where native select was first evaluated and rejected.
